commented_code:
+# Source install of cassandra-driver is broken in setuptools >=82.0.0, see
+# https://github.com/apache/cassandra-python-driver/pull/1268
+setuptools<82.0.0

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -0,0 +1,3 @@
+# Source install of cassandra-driver is broken in setuptools >=82.0.0, see
+# https://github.com/apache/cassandra-python-driver/pull/1268
+setuptools<82.0.0
discussion_comments.comment_body: I feel like we want to be using the most similar build environment possible for different versions of python. We wouldn't want to, for example, create circumstances where we can encounter issues in only 1 of the build environments due to a difference in the setuptools version. The install of cassandra-driver from source is still broken in other python versions, it's just that a pre-built version is already available.  We're just dodging a bullet in CPython 3.11-3.13

discussion_comments.comment_body: Are we sure we want to pin on 3.24? Why?

Thank you for asking: this led me to a deeper investigation on a few warnings one gets when creating the `Session` object (aka connection to Cassandra) which I'm currently diligently mapping with the aim of providing a bit of guidance in the docs.

My (provisional) stance is that indeed 3.25 (currently the latest) is as good as 3.24 - and that actually 3.* would be fine.
I would forbid 4.* for the time being since, according to a warning one sees now, it will require an additional argument to the session constructor that is now optional - an argument I plan to cover in the addition to the docs anyway.
(Note: Cassandra drivers 4.* do not exist yet.)

I will confirm on this thread once I finish my checks.

So I'm leaning on `cassandra-driver>=3.24,<4` at the moment.

Also, I forgot to mention: 3.23 does not seem to be able to connect to AstraDB yet.

My latest commit has `>=3.24,<4` and in the docs I cover the additional configuration needed to silence all warnings from the driver doing guesswork/negotiation on client side. I think that's all good now.
Also I extended the test coverage for the both cluster-creation code pathways (I believe the coverage should now be non-decreasing).
